title: practical english usage - Part 277
content:
Do you know when you'll arrive? Where's the station?

I'm tired.   My father's not very well.

noun/pronoun, etc + (auxiliary) verb with another word. There are two kinds.

Contractions are forms like I've, don't, in which  an auxiliary verb is combined 1  general      rules 337 c ontractions:       I'll, don't,     etc   __________ For abbreviations used in some electronic messages (e.g. hope 2 c u  for hope to see you), ►    290.

RSVP         Please reply (on invitations, from French rpm          revolutions per minute ‘ May  he/she/they rest in peace.’ ) RIP           Rest in Peace (from Latin requiesca(n)t in ; Rd          Road Pl pint post scriptum, ‘ written afterwards’ ) PS           addition to a written message (from Latin Word  Formation and SpellingSection 30 vocabulary •  337    contractions: I'll, don't, etc you've      /ju:v; jəv/                    you have you're      /juə(r)j jɔ:(r): jə(r)/  you are I'd       /aɪd/                              I had/would I'll        /aɪl/                              I will I've       /aɪv/                             I have I'm        /aɪm/                             I am Contraction   Pronunciation  Meaning Strong and weak forms (►   315) are given where appropriate.

4 list   of  contractions They really aren't. No, I haven't.

Negative contractions can come  at the ends of clauses.

Yes, you have.

- I've forgotten.

Yes, you are. (     Fes, you're.) - I'm late.

auxiliary verb) do not normally come at the ends of clauses. Compare: Contractions in the first group (noun / pronoun / question word + 3  position Double contractions are not normally written: she'sn't is impossible.

be more common in northern and Scottish English.) normally used in American English. (Forms with not - e.g. she'd not - tend to most cases in standard southern British English; they are the only forms common. With other verbs, forms with n't (e.g. she hadn't) are more common in he'll not. The  two negative forms of be (e.g. she isn't and she's not) are both we  can say she hadn't or she'd not; for he will not we  can say he won't or Some negative expressions can have two possible contractions. For she had not 2 alternative        contractions pronunciation of informal speech. They  are not generally used in a formal style.

Contractions are common and correct in informal writing; they represent the and won't (= ‘ will not') only have one apostrophe each.

has not = hasn't (     ha's nt). But note that shan't (BrE =  shall not) The  apostrophe (’ ) goes in the same place as the letters that we  leave out: not Jack and I have decided to split up. (     Jack and I've decided ...) Contractions are not usually written with double subjects.

1 wondered what had (/'wotəd/) happened.

'Your mother will (/'mʌðərl/)  be surprised', she said.

in pronunciation.

(especially in British English), even if the words would be contracted unstressed there, but in other cases we  more often write the full forms The  short forms 'll, 'd and  're are commonly written after pronouns and question words, here and now as well as pronouns and unstressed there.

The  short form 's (= is/has) can be written after nouns (including proper names), have when these are not auxiliary verbs.

Contractions are formed with auxiliary verbs, and also with be and sometimes You won't be late, will you?  Can't you swim?
